VI. THE ERPATISTAn AND nIRANGISTAN.

Of all the lost Nasks, the one of which the largest fragments have been preserved is the seventeenth one called the HAsplram. It was composed of sixty-four Fargards, of which two of the first thirty were called Erpatistdn, *the Sacerdotal Code/ and Ntrangistan, * the Ritual Code;' the former dealing chiefly with clerical organisation, and the latter with a portion of the ritual. Their general contents are known from the analysis of the Nasks given in the Dtnkart (VIII, ch. a8, 29; West,Pahlavi Texts, IV, 92-97).

These two Zend treatises were treated like the Vend!d&d, that is to say, were translated and commented on in Pahlavi, at least partially. They have not come to us in any Sdda manuscript, but are to be rcfcovered from their Pahlavi expansion, the so-called Pahlavi Ntrangist&n *, which presents nearly the same aspect as the Pahlavi Vendid&d, that is to say, it contains the Zend original text with a Pahlavi translation, and a lengthy commentary, in which latter many connected questions are treated and a considerable number of Zend quotations from other Nasks are adduced. The first thi^g to do is to distinguish what belongs to the principal text, which is the object of the commentary, and what are the Zend quotations adduced from elsewhere by the commentator. The distinction of the two components is easily seen, as the principal text is always accompanied by a translation, whereas the quotations are not. They are either formulas recited during the performance of the ceremonies, or texts adduced as demonstrative or explanatory of such or such statement*. These quotations once removed, there remains a continuous text which answers closely to the analysis in the Dinkart. But a comparison with that analysis, as well as internal evidence, shows that only a part of the original text is preserved, and that ^ It has been long known under that title, but ought to be called ' Erpatistdn and Ntrangist&n/ ■ They are adduced with the uniform words . . . min . . . padt&k yahv0n6t, ' it appears from the passage : . . .'
VI. ERPATIStAn and NiRANGISTAN. 3OI - —

the Pahlavi manuscript, as it has come to us, is the juxtaposition of portions of two independent books, the Erpatist&n and the Ntrangistin proper, the beginning and end of both being lost. In other terms, it contains a part in the middle of the £rpatist£n ^ and the greater part of the NfrangistSn, the end of the latter being lost as well as a short passage at its beginning \ All the manuscripts of the Nirangist&n, known to be in existence, present the same juxtaposition, as they are descended from one and the same manuscript, of which the copyist, having in his hands a fragment of the £rpatistfln and a more complete Ntrangistdn, copied the two as one and the same book, which took the name of the larger fragment. This leaves room to hope for the further discovery of older independent manuscripts of either book.
Here is a summary of the matter treated of, with references to the analysis in the Dinkart : —
Fargard I. First Part (Fragment of the ErpatistAn).
I. §§ 1-9. The priest on duty out (Dfnkart VIII, ch. 28, § 2 ?).
II. §§"10-18. The student priest (Dk. ibid. § 3?).
Second Part (Nirangistan proper).
I. §§ 19-27. The Z6t and the Rfispf (Dk. VIII, ch. 29, § i).
II. § 28. The DaHin (Dk. ibid. § 2).
III. §§ 29, 30. Strong drink forbidden during the sacrifice Pl^. § 3).
IV. §§ 31-37. The recitation of the Gdthas (Dk. § 4).
V. §§ 38-40. The sacrifice performed by a Z6t, or a RAspt, in a state of sin (Dk. §§ 5, 6).
Fargard II.
I. f§ 41-45. The celebration of the G4h4nbdrs (Dk. §§ 7, 8).
II. §§ 46-51. The limits of the several Gahs (§ 46, Gdh ' §§ 1-18 belong to the Erpatistdn.

' Of the twenty-five paragraphs in the Dinkart analysis, part of § I, the whole of §§ 2-16, and part of § 17 are represented in the extant Nirangistin. But one must bear in mind that the analysis in the Dfnkart was not based on the Zend Nasks, but on their Pahlavi commentaries, so that it refers occasionally to matter not treated of in the S&da text.

一名男子藉由眼光與言語，得以成功地統領其世間財富——無生命與有生命的，動產與不動產——當他藉由讓熔化的金屬澆淋在自己胸膛上，來駁斥異端邪說並彰顯正統教義之時。
這個片段似乎涉及訴訟當事人之一提出訴諸神判的提議（參見《法朗格文集》第15則的片段）。
他以眼光監督他的無生命財產，以言語監督他的有生命財產。
六、埃爾帕蒂斯坦與迪蘭吉斯坦

在所有失傳的那斯克中，保存下來最多片段的是第十七部，稱為《哈斯皮蘭》（HAsplram）。它由六十四個法耶德（Fargards）組成，其中前三十部中的兩部分別稱為《祭司法典》（Erpatistdn）和《儀軌法典》（Ntrangistan），前者主要涉及教職組織，後者則涉及儀軌的某些部分。它們的一般內容可從《丁卡爾特》（Dtnkart，VIII，第28、29章；West, *Pahlavi Texts*, IV, 92-97）中對那斯克的分析得知。

這兩部瑣羅亞斯德教經典的處理方式如同《萬德達》（Vendidād），亦即被翻譯並以帕拉維文評註，至少部分如此。它們沒有以任何《薩達》（Sdda）手稿的形式傳至我們手中，而是需要從其帕拉維文擴展本中復原，即所謂的帕拉維文《尼朗吉斯坦》（Pahlavi Ntrangistān）*，其呈現的面貌幾乎與帕拉維文《萬德達》相同，亦即包含瑣羅亞斯德原文及帕拉維文譯文，以及冗長的評述，在後者中許多相關問題得到探討，並引用了來自其他《納斯克》（Nasks）的相當數量的瑣羅亞斯德文引文。首要任務是區分什麼屬於主要正文（即評述的對象），什麼是評論者從他處引用的瑣羅亞斯德文引文。這兩個組成部分的區分易於辨識，因為主要正文總是附有譯文，而引文則否。它們要麼是在儀式舉行期間誦讀的公式，要麼是為論證或闡明某某陳述而引用的文本*。移除這些引文後，剩下一部連貫的文本，與《丁卡爾特》（Dinkart）中的分析高度相符。然而，與該分析進行比較，以及內在證據表明，原文本只有一部分得以保存，且 ^ 長期以來其名稱如此，但應被稱作「厄帕提斯坦與尼朗吉斯坦」（'Erpatistān and Ntrangistān'）。■ 它們以統一的詞語引用…… min …… padāk yahvōnēt，「從該段落可見：……」
VI. 埃爾帕蒂斯坦與尼蘭吉斯坦 301

帕拉維手稿按其傳存至今的形貌，乃是兩部獨立著作的片段之並置，即《厄爾帕蒂斯坦》（Erpatistān）與《涅蘭吉斯坦》（Nīrangistān）本身，兩書的開端與結尾均已散佚。換言之，該手稿包含了《厄爾帕蒂斯坦》中間部分的片段，以及《涅蘭吉斯坦》的大部分內容，後者的結尾與開端的短篇段落同樣已失。現存已知的所有《涅蘭吉斯坦》手稿都呈現相同的並置現象，因其皆源於同一份手稿；該手稿的抄寫者手中持有《厄爾帕蒂斯坦》的殘本及較為完整的《涅蘭吉斯坦》，遂將兩書並為一書抄錄，並以較大的片段之名命名此合卷。這使人有理由寄望於未來能進一步發現兩部著作各自的更古老的獨立手稿。
以下是所述事項的摘要，附有《丹卡特》(Dinkart) 中分析部分的參考資料：——
法爾加德第一篇 第一部分（埃帕蒂斯坦的殘篇）
I. §§ 1-9. 當值的祭司外出（Dênkart VIII, ch. 28, § 2 ?）。
II. §§ 10-18. 學生祭司（Dk. ibid. § 3?）。
第二部分（正式的《淨火教經》）。
I. §§ 19-27. 祆教與梨俱吠陀（Dk. VIII, ch. 29, § i）。
三、第29、30節。獻祭期間禁止飲用烈酒（參見《梨俱吠陀》第3頌）。
IV. §§ 31-37. 迦他經文的誦讀（Dk. § 4）
V. §§ 38-40. 由祆教徒（Zōt）或祭司（Rāspt）在有罪狀態下進行的祭祀（《波斯法典》§§ 5, 6）。
法爾加德二
I. f§ 41-45. 格哈斯特里斯（Gṛhabhdrs）的慶典（Dk. §§ 7, 8）。
（§ 46, Gdh ' §§ 1-18 屬於 Erpatistdn）
在敦卡爾特（Dinkart）分析的二十五段落中，第一段的一部分、第二至十六段的全部，以及第十七段的一部分在現存的尼朗吉斯廷（Nirangistin）中有所代表。但必須銘記在心的是，敦卡爾特中的分析並非以贊德‧納斯克（Zend Nasks）為基礎，而是以其帕赫拉維註疏（Pahlavi commentaries）為基礎，因此它偶爾涉及索達（Sāda）文本中未曾論及的內容。
